claim: REQ-002 acceptance requires the main-side observer (REQ-001, in src/main) to import buildDecisionQueue from src/shared/decision-queue.ts, but frozen TEST-358 (tests/renderer/app-queue-wiring.test.ts:54-55) scans every src/main and src/preload file with the regex /decision-?queue|DecisionQueue|queueOpen/i and asserts zero matches. Both the imported symbol name and the module path decision-queue match that regex, so the observer file trips TEST-358 -- there is no way to import the required shared selector into src/main without matching. This contradicts REQ-013 MUST (trips no shipped frozen guard); REQ-013 inventory omits TEST-358 entirely.
fix: Inventory TEST-358 in REQ-013 and specify its CONV-019 sanctioned amendment: narrow the regex to F6 renderer-specific tokens (DecisionQueuePanel / setQueueOpen / queueOpen) so a legitimate main-side import of the shared buildDecisionQueue selector no longer false-trips it, updated atomically at the tests phase. propose CONV: a directory-scoped absence/scope-guard source scan MUST key its regex on the feature-specific surface (component/handler names), never on a shared module name or import path, so a later feature that legitimately imports that shared module into the scanned directory does not false-trip the guard.
resolution: Resolved by spec rev 2026-07-02 (REQ-013): TEST-358 is now inventoried and its CONV-019 supersession scheduled at the tests phase, narrowing the offenders regex to /DecisionQueuePanel|queueOpen/i. Verified against the frozen text (app-queue-wiring.test.ts:40-56): the narrowed regex still catches a planted main-side DecisionQueuePanel or queueOpen/setQueueOpen leak (the guard's real intent per its header lines 1-8) yet permits the observer's buildDecisionQueue import AND the @shared/decision-queue module path (neither matches). Re-grep of tests/** confirms TEST-358 is the ONLY main/preload directory-scan carrying decision-queue tokens; the other hits (statusbar-queue-toggle.test.ts:38, registry-slice.test.ts, decision-queue-build.test.ts) scan renderer/shared source only. Inventory now complete.

claim: The production opt-in gate shouldNotify(projectRoot) is synchronous (returns boolean, Public interface), but its source quick.orkyNeedsYouNotifications is written renderer-side via fire-and-forget quickSave (register-workspaces.ts:22) with no main-side broadcast, and QuickStore keeps no in-memory cache (load() is async and re-reads disk each call). A synchronous gate can therefore only read a value cached at startup, and nothing specified refreshes it. No REQ pins a live-refresh mechanism, and REQ-005 acceptance exercises only the injected stub and the persistence round-trip -- never that toggling the setting at runtime actually mutes the observer within a bounded time. A stale-until-restart implementation passes every stated acceptance, so the sole user-facing control (the mute) may not take effect until the app restarts, defeating the concept never-spammy / opt-in-respect goal.
fix: Specify a bounded live-refresh mechanism compatible with the synchronous gate (e.g. the observer re-reads quick.json into a cached flag on each onSnapshot, or a quickSave-triggered main-side callback updates it) and add an acceptance asserting that a runtime toggle to false stops notifications within a bounded window (next snapshot/save), not only that the injected stub gates.
resolution: Resolved by REQ-005 Wiring + Upstream note 3: a main-side in-memory mirror initialized from await quick.load() at startup and refreshed synchronously from the full QuickStore payload flowing through the existing quickSave handler; the synchronous production shouldNotify reads the mirror. Verified: register-workspaces.ts:22 handler receives the full data:QuickStore payload, so onQuickSave(data) can set the mirror to data.orkyNeedsYouNotifications !== false. No bypass save-path exists -- every QuickStore.save routes through CH.quickSave (preload saveQuick at index.ts:57; renderer store.ts:50 debounced saveQuick sends get().quick); the only other .save( calls (orky-registry.ts:143,174) target the OrkyRegistryStore/orky-registry.json, a different store. Live-refresh acceptance now exercises the REAL handler+mirror+production shouldNotify (mute on next transition, no restart). Init race is benign: the default-on !== false idiom reads an uninitialized/absent mirror as ENABLED, the correct default.

claim: REQ-004 digest count is ambiguous and untestable as written. The normative clause says the digest names N = the count of distinct projects coalesced in the window, but the acceptance says naming count 6 (or the coalesced count) -- two contradictory correct values (6 = all projects in the window including the 3 already shown as individual toasts, vs 3 = only those coalesced beyond DIGEST_THRESHOLD). Picking 6 double-counts projects already surfaced individually. Separately, the throttle model mixes a rolling window (rolling COALESCE_WINDOW_MS window) with a tumbling one (flushed no later than COALESCE_WINDOW_MS after the window opened); these are different rate models and change the per-window cap and flush timing, so the determinism claim is not fully pinned.
fix: Pick one digest-count definition (recommend N = distinct projects coalesced beyond the individual-toast threshold, excluding those already shown) and have the acceptance assert that single value; pick and state one window model (tumbling window opened at first transition, flushed at open + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS) consistently in both the normative text and the acceptance.
resolution: Resolved by REQ-004 (rev 2026-07-02): a single TUMBLING window model is pinned (opens at first transition while no window open, closes at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS, next transition after close opens a fresh window -- never rolling), stated identically in Resolved #6, the determinism concern, and REQ-004. The digest count is now single-valued: N = distinct projectRoots among buffered transitions NOT already surfaced as an individual toast this window. Acceptance asserts count 3 (never 6) for 6 distinct projects, the edge at exactly DIGEST_THRESHOLD (3 transitions -> 3 individual, 0 digest, empty buffer -> no digest), the already-shown-project exclusion, and trailing flush at window close / dispose. Both count and window decision are pure functions of injected timestamps + identities.

claim: REQ-013 claims to enumerate every frozen SCHEMA_VERSION .toBe(8) pin (TEST-008/020/038/087/344/375) and that the spec stated grep pattern reproduces this inventory, but a SCHEMA_VERSION sweep of tests/** also surfaces tests/shared/minimize-persistence.test.ts:30 (TEST-001) expect(SCHEMA_VERSION).toBe(8), which is omitted from the list. Because F13 does not bump SCHEMA_VERSION the pin stays green so no test breaks, but the exhaustive / grep-verified claim is false (CONV-023).
fix: Add TEST-001 (tests/shared/minimize-persistence.test.ts:30) to the REQ-013 .toBe(8) inventory, or state the exact grep pattern and scope and confirm it reproduces the full set including TEST-001.
resolution: Resolved by REQ-013: TEST-001 (minimize-persistence.test.ts:30) is now listed; the inventory is the COMPLETE seven expect(SCHEMA_VERSION).toBe(8) constant pins (TEST-001/008/020/038/087/344/375). Independent re-grep of tests/** for .toBe(8) reproduces exactly these seven SCHEMA_VERSION-constant pins at the cited lines. The only other literal .toBe(8) hits on the schema version are persisted-value pins -- e2e/orky-pane.spec.ts:120 (parsed.schemaVersion) and orky-pane-migration.test.ts:62 (JSON.parse(s1).schemaVersion) -- which do not carry the SCHEMA_VERSION token, are correctly outside the scoped constant-pin claim, and stay green because F13 adds no bump. CONV-023 exhaustiveness satisfied for the stated scope.

claim: Tumbling-window digest never emits on quiet-time elapse: manageWindow only closes/rolls the window from within admit(), and register.ts subscribes onSnapshot with no timer, so a burst of >=DIGEST_THRESHOLD projects followed by quiet leaves the digest pending INDEFINITELY instead of firing at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS as REQ-004 specifies. The 51 frozen tests exercised window-close via a subsequent admit()/flush()/dispose() but never a real elapsed-time close, so the gap shipped green. [MERGE: independently confirmed by quality(007), determinism(008), ux(009), devils-advocate(013) — five-lens convergence.]
fix: Inject a fakeable timer scheduler (setTimer/clearTimer deps) onto the notifier; schedule the close-window/flush at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS when a window opens, clear it on flush()/dispose(); register.ts supplies real timers. Add a tests-phase vector: burst → advance the fake clock past the window with NO further transition → digest fires exactly once; and dispose clears the pending timer.
verified_by: codex-cross-check (filed by coordinator after line-level verification at :157-165; codex sandbox lock EPERM)
resolution: RESOLVED against current code. The notifier now arms a real window-close driver: openWindow() calls armFlushTimer() (orky-needs-you-notifier.ts:197,205-213) which schedules deps.setTimer(closeWindow, COALESCE_WINDOW_MS) at window open; a one-shot callback nulls its own handle then closeWindow()s. Pinned RED->GREEN by TEST-575 (quiet-elapse: advance the fake clock past the window with NO further onSnapshot -> digest fires EXACTLY once, never re-fires) and TEST-576 (dispose before fire clears the timer, timerCount()===0, no double-fire); frozen TEST-555 (no timer after dispose) stays green. EXACTLY-ONCE / no-double verified three ways: (a) the timer callback is one-shot and removes its own handle; (b) a later transition that rolls the window goes through manageWindow->closeWindow->clearFlushTimer (:186-189,228-229,216-221) which cancels the pending real timer before opening the fresh window, so the elapsed-timer path and the roll path cannot both emit; (c) after a quiet-elapse close, windowOpenedAt is null so the next transition opens a fresh window rather than re-emitting. dispose()->closeWindow()->clearFlushTimer() clears the handle (no leak). PRODUCTION SILENT-DROP RULED OUT: the timer deps are OPTIONAL only to let the frozen mirror test (orky-needs-you-mirror.test.ts TEST-561) construct a timer-less notifier that fires ONLY individual toasts (never buffers a digest, so the lazy fallback is never reached there); the real app ALWAYS supplies them -- register.ts:120,138-139 wires setTimer:(fn,ms)=>setTimeout(fn,ms) / clearTimer:h=>clearTimeout(h), structurally pinned by TEST-577 (asserts register.ts contains setTimer:/clearTimer:/setTimeout(/clearTimeout(). All 28 notifier + 5 loopback + 2 mirror vectors green.

claim: pane-reveal.ts's paneMatchesFor()+focusProjectPane() duplicates -- rather than reuses -- DecisionQueuePanel.tsx's private paneMatches useMemo + focusProject: both independently walk every workspace/pane, call matchPaneRootFromCandidates(selectPaneCandidates(...), memberRoots, {caseFold}) to resolve each pane's member root, then selectMruPane + setActive/setFocusedPane/requestPaneFocus to focus the pick. REQ-006's acceptance ('reuses the shared matcher rather than a fork') is satisfied only at the low-level primitive layer (matchPaneRootFromCandidates/selectPaneCandidates/selectMruPane/caseFoldFromPlatform ARE imported from @shared/decision-queue in both files); the orchestration layer -- the per-workspace/per-pane walk and the MRU-pick-then-dispatch tail -- is written twice with no shared source of truth. 03-plan.md TASK-010 risk note 4 explicitly anticipated this and called a duplicate implementation 'out of bounds', directing a 'small refactor (export the matcher call, or a shared helper)' instead. Verified this was not force-by a frozen test: DecisionQueuePanel.tsx's own frozen structural tests (TEST-344/345/346/347/348/349/350/351/352/353 in decision-queue-panel-structure.test.ts, TEST-370/371 in decision-queue-loopback.test.ts) place no constraint that would break from factoring the selectMruPane+setActive+setFocusedPane+requestPaneFocus tail into one shared helper called by both DecisionQueuePanel.focusProject and pane-reveal.ts's focusProjectPane; only TEST-370 pins that the literal substring selectPaneCandidates( must remain inside DecisionQueuePanel.tsx's own source (its per-render paneMatches walk, used separately for the hasPane render check), which constrains full extraction of that half but not the MRU-pick/dispatch tail. The two copies must now be kept in sync by hand (e.g. a future selectPaneCandidates signal addition, per CONV-024/026's staleness-propagation class) or they will silently diverge.
fix: Factor the MRU-select + focus-dispatch tail (selectMruPane + setActive/setFocusedPane/requestPaneFocus) into one exported helper in pane-reveal.ts, e.g. focusMruPaneMatch(matching, paneFocusSeq, {setActive, setFocusedPane}), and have both DecisionQueuePanel.tsx's focusProject and pane-reveal.ts's focusProjectPane call it with their own locally-derived matching array (each keeps its own selectPaneCandidates walk since DecisionQueuePanel.tsx also needs paneMatches for its hasPane render check and TEST-370 pins that call site inside the panel file).
resolution: RESOLVED against current code, exactly per the prescribed fix. The MRU-pick + focus-dispatch tail is now ONE exported helper: pane-reveal.ts:43-56 focusMruPaneMatch(matching, paneFocusSeq, {setActive,setFocusedPane}) does selectMruPane + setActive + setFocusedPane + requestPaneFocus. pane-reveal.ts:61-64 focusProjectPane routes through it, and DecisionQueuePanel.tsx:4 imports it from './pane-reveal' and :84 focusProject calls it -- no forked MRU/dispatch copy. Pinned by TEST-582 (export exists; >=2 references in pane-reveal; panel imports+calls it). No import cycle: pane-reveal.ts imports only @shared/decision-queue, ./terminal-registry (a leaf imperative-hook registry), and type-only ../store/types; it does NOT import DecisionQueuePanel (verified by grep -- the edge is one-directional panel->pane-reveal). The per-workspace/per-pane walk stays duplicated BY DESIGN (paneMatchesFor in pane-reveal vs the paneMatches useMemo in the panel) because frozen TEST-370 pins selectPaneCandidates( inside the panel file and the panel also needs paneMatches for its hasPane render check -- exactly the scope carve-out the fix specified; TEST-370 stays green (confirmed). Contract satisfied: the tail now has a single source of truth.

claim: REQ-004 requires 'A pending digest MUST be flushed exactly at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS (window close) and on dispose, whichever comes first, so a trailing burst is never silently dropped.' The shipped OrkyNeedsYouNotifier has NO timer of any kind (its own header comment states 'no timers are ever armed'; grep confirms no setTimeout/setInterval in orky-needs-you-notifier.ts or in its register.ts composition wiring). The window-close flush is checked ONLY lazily, inside manageWindow(), which runs ONLY when a NEW candidate arrives via onSnapshot -- and onSnapshot is driven solely by orkyRegistry.onSnapshot, which fires ONLY on a genuine membership/status change (verified: no setInterval/poll in orky-registry.ts). TASK-003 in 03-plan.md explicitly sanctioned this lazy-only choice as one of two implementer options ('checked lazily ... OR eagerly via an internal boundary check -- implementer's choice, but MUST be exact-once'), but the lazy option has no delivery guarantee: if the LAST needs-you transition of a session opens/rolls a window and buffers 1+ digest entries, and no OTHER tracked project's status changes again before the app quits, the buffered digest sits in bufferedRoots forever -- it is only flushed by dispose() at app teardown, at which point Notification.show() races app/process shutdown and may never actually render. This scenario is not a corner case for this feature: REQ-001's flagship case is a PANE-LESS project with no open pane and (by construction) no heartbeat/pty activity of its own, which is exactly the class of project least likely to trigger another recompute() before the window boundary passes. No test exercises this: the pure suite (orky-needs-you-notifier.test.ts) only proves flush()/dispose() work when called directly (which the production wiring never does at the boundary time); the e2e suite (orky-notify.spec.ts, TEST-573/574) never drives more than DIGEST_THRESHOLD transitions to actually produce a buffered digest, let alone lets the session go quiet past COALESCE_WINDOW_MS to check whether the digest is ever delivered without further aggregate activity or app quit. The 'never silently dropped' guarantee is therefore not actually enforced by the shipped code path in the realistic quiet-session case.
fix: Arm a bounded eager check for the window boundary: either (a) the composition root (register.ts) schedules a single setTimeout on window-open (read from the notifier, e.g. via a new onWindowOpen(ms) hook or by having the notifier accept a scheduleFlush ambient) that calls notifier.flush() at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS, cleared/rearmed on each roll and cleared on dispose; or (b) add an eager boundary check inside admit()/onSnapshot itself is insufficient alone (it only re-checks on the NEXT candidate) -- a real timer is required for the no-further-activity case. Add a main-integration or e2e test that produces a buffered digest, advances real or fake time past COALESCE_WINDOW_MS with NO further onSnapshot call, and asserts the digest is still delivered before dispose.
resolution: Merged into FINDING-005 (same digest-flush-timer defect, independently filed by a different lens in the concurrent fan-out; FINDING-005 is the canonical tracked entry).

claim: REQ-004 MUST (a pending digest is flushed EXACTLY at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS (window close) and on dispose, whichever comes first, so a trailing burst is never silently dropped) is not honored in production. The observer arms no timer by design AND onSnapshot early-returns before manageWindow whenever candidates.length===0 (orky-needs-you-notifier.ts:131), so the open window is closed/flushed ONLY by a LATER snapshot that itself carries a NEW needs-you transition, or by flush(), or by dispose(). The production composition root (register.ts:120-135) injects now: Date.now() but wires NO periodic flush and calls flush() nowhere; the sole driver is dispose() at onAllWindowsClosed (register.ts:159/161). Consequence: after a burst of more than DIGEST_THRESHOLD transitions, when the human is away (exactly what this feature exists for) and the watched trees go quiet, the coalesced digest (N projects need you) is stranded until the next NEW transition or app-close teardown; it is NOT flushed at window close and is effectively silently dropped for the session (a shutdown-time Notification is unreliable/useless). Steady-state or resolution snapshots do NOT flush it either, because zero-candidate snapshots return before manageWindow. Determinism corollary: the digest delivery time becomes a function of unrelated future snapshot arrivals, not of the transition timestamps + injected clock REQ-004 promises. Test-adequacy gap: no frozen test exercises the pure-time/quiet-tail flush — TEST-542 forces window close by feeding another transition, TEST-543/554 by flush()/dispose() — so the flushed-exactly-at-window-close MUST is asserted only where an external event performs the flush.
fix: Provision the production window-close driver that no REQ/TASK currently assigns: after an onSnapshot that opens a window, arm one setTimeout(() => needsYouNotifier.flush(), COALESCE_WINDOW_MS) in the composition root (cleared/rescheduled on window roll and on dispose so TEST-555 no-timer-after-dispose stays green), and add an acceptance that a trailing buffered transition emits its digest at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS with NO subsequent transition and NO explicit flush()/dispose(). If lazy-only flush is truly the intended contract, instead amend REQ-004 to drop (flushed exactly at window close ... never silently dropped) and state the digest fires on the next transition/flush/dispose (CONV-018: reconcile the frozen spec with the shipped behavior rather than leaving the MUST unmet). propose CONV: a coalescing/debounce buffer whose spec promises a flush at a wall-clock deadline MUST be driven by an actual scheduled flush at that deadline, never only lazily on the next inbound event, or the deadline guarantee is void whenever the input stream goes quiet.
resolution: Merged into FINDING-005 (same digest-flush-timer defect, independently filed by a different lens in the concurrent fan-out; FINDING-005 is the canonical tracked entry).

claim: REQ-004 MUST: a pending coalesced digest 'MUST be flushed exactly at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS (window close) ... so a trailing burst is never silently dropped.' The implementation arms NO timer (impl comment: 'No timers are ever armed'). OrkyNeedsYouNotifier.closeWindow()/flush() runs only (a) lazily on the next onSnapshot that crosses the boundary (manageWindow roll) or (b) via dispose() at app quit (register.ts:159). 03-plan.md:134 explicitly reinterprets the window-close flush as 'checked lazily on the next onSnapshot/flush/dispose call', contradicting the spec's 'exactly at'. Consequence: when more than DIGEST_THRESHOLD projects transition into needs-you within one 4s window and then nothing else changes (the registry emits no further snapshot), the coalesced 'N projects need you' digest is NEVER delivered at the 4s mark; it fires only if a later unrelated needs-you transition happens, or (stale) at app quit via dispose. The trailing burst is silently withheld, defeating REQ-004's coalesce guarantee and the never-drop clause. Tests are complicit: TEST-542 exercises window close only via a subsequent transition; TEST-543/554 only via manual flush()/dispose() — no test proves a wall-clock window-close flush with no following onSnapshot.
fix: Give the tumbling window a real close trigger in production: arm a timer (composition root or observer) for windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS that calls flush()/closeWindow(), cleared on window close and on dispose (consistent with REQ-012 'no timer remains armed after dispose'). Add a fake-timer behavioral test asserting a trailing buffered transition fires its digest at the wall-clock window-close time with NO subsequent onSnapshot (not only via manual flush()/dispose() or a next-transition roll).
resolution: Merged into FINDING-005 (same digest-flush-timer defect, independently filed by a different lens in the concurrent fan-out; FINDING-005 is the canonical tracked entry).

claim: REQ-007 requires a pane-less notification click to open the drawer 'scrolled to that project's group'. The scroll behavior is pinned only by a source grep (TEST-570 asserts the strings scrollIntoView|decision-queue-group- exist) and the e2e (TEST-573) only asserts the drawer panel becomes visible — neither verifies the CORRECT group is actually scrolled into view. Meanwhile revealQueueGroup() schedules a single requestAnimationFrame immediately after setQueueOpen(true) conditionally mounts DecisionQueuePanel (App.tsx: {queueOpen && <DecisionQueuePanel/>}); on the primary closed->open path the target group element may not be committed to the DOM by that one rAF, so querySelectorAll returns nothing and the scroll silently no-ops. The requirement's 'land where the human can act' outcome is thus unproven and possibly broken on first open.
fix: Add a behavioral (jsdom/e2e) test that clicks a pane-less notification while the drawer is CLOSED and asserts the specific decision-queue-group-<root> element is scrolled into view (spy scrollIntoView on that group). Make revealQueueGroup robust to conditional-mount timing (retry across a couple of frames, a mount effect in the panel, or observe the group's appearance) rather than a single rAF.
resolution: RESOLVED against current code. revealQueueGroup (pane-reveal.ts:74-85) now RETRIES across frames instead of a single give-up rAF: a tick() increments a frame counter, queries [data-testid^="decision-queue-group-"], scrolls the element whose dataset.projectRoot === root and RETURNS (scroll exactly once), else re-schedules via requestAnimationFrame ONLY while frames < REVEAL_MAX_FRAMES (=30). Bounded-termination verified: the retry is gated purely on the frame counter, so if the group never mounts the loop stops cleanly after 30 frames with no scroll and NO infinite rAF (no unbounded scheduling, no busy-wait). Pinned by TEST-583 (fake rAF + a document whose target group appears only on the 3rd query -> scrolled exactly once, queries >= 3). Exact-group match preserved (dataset compare, no selector interpolation). Non-blocking MEDIUM cleared.

claim: For a pane running Claude on an Orky project, one 'the agent stopped, come look' moment can fire TWO OS notifications: 0004's per-pane 'X is waiting for you' (runtime-slice.ts:28-31, on the AI-session busy->quiet flip, gated on per-pane alerts + document unfocused) AND F13's app-wide '<proj> needs you' (on the orky needsHuman transition). The two systems have independent opt-ins and no cross-coordination, so while the window is unfocused a user can get duplicate toasts for effectively the same event. The spec (upstream note 1) acknowledges the two are distinct signals but never addresses the resulting double-notify from the user's seat.
fix: Decide and document the coexistence: either accept it explicitly (they convey different facts) or suppress the F13 app-wide toast for a project whose needs-you moment was already surfaced by an open pane's 0004 per-pane notification. propose CONV: two independent notification paths that can fire for the same user-attention event MUST have a stated coexistence/dedupe policy so a single moment cannot emit duplicate OS toasts.
note: CONFIRMED accepted residual (re-verification 2026-07-02). Per ESC-001 (human, delegated) this LOW double-notify is a deliberate, documented residual: 0004's per-pane and F13's app-wide toasts convey distinct facts and are deliberately left uncoordinated pending a future cross-notification-coordination feature. Documented in 04-tests.md:234-241 ('FINDING-011 — ACCEPTED residual (NOT pinned)'); intentionally carries no TEST id so a premature dedupe does not over-constrain the future design. Left open (residual, non-blocking) by design, not an unaddressed defect.

claim: Notification title/body interpolate the project basename and the feature slug (individualCopy at orky-needs-you-notifier.ts:187-194), both derived from on-disk .orky project/feature directory names. On Linux the notification server (libnotify/Pango) interprets a limited markup set in the body (<b>, <i>, <a href>, entities); a crafted directory/slug name from an adopted/cloned repo could inject markup, or on strict servers an unbalanced tag can cause the body to be dropped/mangled — degrading the 'specific and actionable' copy (CONV-001). No script execution is possible (a notification is not a web context) and macOS/Windows render title/body as plain text, so impact is low and local-input scoped. The renderer focus path is NOT a concern: revealQueueGroup matches on el.dataset.projectRoot === root (exact compare, no selector interpolation) and read-only scope holds (REQ-008 verified: observer/sinks do no .orky write, no action, no registry mutation).
fix: Escape or strip Pango-significant characters (& < >) from the interpolated project basename and feature slug before building the Notification body on Linux (or entity-encode them). Add a copy test with a name containing < & > asserting the rendered body stays intact/plain.
resolution: RESOLVED against current code. escapeMarkup (orky-needs-you-notifier.ts:68-70) entity-encodes the Pango-significant chars with the ampersand FIRST: .replace(/&/g,'&').replace(//g,'>') -- correct order so the entities are not double-encoded. It is applied to BOTH on-disk-derived interpolants in individualCopy (:243-244): the project basename and the feature slug. The reason phrasing is a fixed vocabulary (reasonPhrase, not attacker-influenced) and the digest body interpolates only the numeric projectCount, so no other injection surface exists. Pinned by TEST-581 (a basename a&b + slug pwn: no raw tag survives, no bare non-entity ampersand, informative text 'pwn' preserved). Plain/intact on macOS/Windows (verbatim render) and safe on Linux/Pango. Non-blocking LOW cleared.

claim: REQ-004 MUST: 'A pending digest MUST be flushed exactly at windowOpenedAt + COALESCE_WINDOW_MS (window close) and on dispose ... so a trailing burst is never silently dropped.' The pure OrkyNeedsYouNotifier deliberately has NO timers (correct for determinism/testability): its window-close flush is LAZY — closeWindow() only runs when a SUBSEQUENT onSnapshot arrives with now() past the boundary (manageWindow), or when flush()/dispose() is called. But the composition root (register.ts) wires NO driver for the window-close leg: it subscribes onSnapshot (line 136) and only ever calls flush indirectly via needsYouNotifier.dispose() in the disposer (line 159, i.e. app shutdown). No setTimeout arms a flush at windowOpenedAt+4000 (grep of src/main confirms zero flush/timer callers for the notifier). Consequence: when a burst of >DIGEST_THRESHOLD transitions arrives and the registry then goes QUIET — the NORMAL case for needs-you, since a stalled/escalated project stops touching .orky while it waits on the human, emitting no further snapshot — the coalesced digest for the 4th+ projects NEVER fires during the session (it flushes only on the next unrelated transition, arbitrarily late, or on dispose during shutdown when Notification display is unreliable). The entire REQ-004 digest mechanism (8 tests) is thus non-functional in production for burst-then-idle, and the buffered projects are silently dropped — exactly what the MUST forbids. No test catches this: the pure suite calls flush()/feeds a later snapshot (TEST-542/543/554) and the wiring suite only asserts register.ts contains '.dispose()' (TEST-564) — nothing asserts a production window-boundary flush.
fix: Give the composition root a real driver for the window-close flush: when the notifier opens a window, arm a single setTimeout(COALESCE_WINDOW_MS) in register.ts that calls needsYouNotifier.flush(), re-arming while a window stays open and clearing on dispose. The pure module currently exposes no 'window opened' signal, so add a minimal seam (e.g. an injected onWindowOpen()/scheduleFlush callback, or an armed:boolean the root can poll) — keep the timer OUT of the notifier so TEST-555 (no timer after dispose, tested on the notifier in isolation) stays valid. Add a main-integration test that, with a burst-then-quiet sequence and a fake timer, asserts the digest fires at windowOpenedAt+COALESCE_WINDOW_MS with NO subsequent snapshot. propose CONV: a coalescing/rate-limit window whose spec requires a flush at a wall-clock boundary MUST have a production-side timer that fires that flush; a pure lazy-flush-on-next-event module needs the composition root to arm a real timer at window-open, or a trailing burst is dropped whenever the event source goes quiet.
resolution: Merged into FINDING-005 (same digest-flush-timer defect, independently filed by a different lens in the concurrent fan-out; FINDING-005 is the canonical tracked entry).

claim: keyOf composes the dedupe identity as [projectRoot, featureSlug, String(reason)].join(newline) and the doc comment asserts (the newline separator cannot occur in a root/slug/reason, so distinct identities never collide). That collision-safety claim is false. Only reason is truly newline-free (the shared selector constrains it to escalation/stalled/human-review/null). A POSIX path segment may legally contain a newline byte (only slash and NUL are forbidden in a filename), and featureSlug is not constrained by the selector to exclude newlines, so two distinct (root,slug,reason) tuples whose newline-joined form coincides collapse to ONE dedupe key (e.g. root /a + slug (b-newline-c) vs root (/a-newline-b) + slug c both yield /a NL b NL c). A collision false-dedupes a genuine needs-you transition (silently suppressing a notification, REQ-003) and thereby breaks REQ-002 same-sequence->same-notifications for such inputs. Practical impact is LOW (impossible on Windows paths; pathological on POSIX) and the collision is itself deterministic, so this is not a nondeterminism per se; the defect is that the guaranteed collision-safety the comment promises is not actually held, and the chosen printable separator is strictly weaker than a control-char delimiter that would make the claim true.
fix: Use a separator that provably cannot appear in any path/slug/reason: NUL (\0) is disallowed in every filesystem path and is the natural collision-safe delimiter the comment already describes, or encode the key structurally (JSON.stringify([root,slug,reason]) or length-prefixed segments). Then make the doc comment match the guarantee that actually holds. propose CONV: a composite dedup/identity key built by joining filesystem- or user-derived string fields MUST use a separator that provably cannot occur in any field (NUL, or a structural/length-prefixed encoding), never a printable or whitespace character an OS permits inside a path or name.
resolution: RESOLVED against current code. keyOf (orky-needs-you-notifier.ts:58-60) is now STRUCTURAL: JSON.stringify([item.projectRoot, item.featureSlug, String(item.status?.reason)]) -- provably collision-free because every field is quote-escaped and the field boundaries are unambiguous, so no in-field character (newline, or any other byte an OS permits inside a path/slug) can forge a boundary. No dedupe behavior change for normal keys: distinct tuples still map 1:1 to distinct strings and identical tuples to the same string, so all dedupe-lifecycle vectors stay green (TEST-534/535/536/537 steady-state / reason-change / resolve / vanished-prune). Pinned by TEST-579 (structural: no .join('\n'); source contains JSON.stringify or a NUL delimiter) and the GREEN distinctness fence TEST-580 (two features under one root whose slugs differ only by an embedded '\n' stay two distinct toasts, never collapse to one). Non-blocking LOW cleared.

claim: REQ-005 MUST: the observer 'MUST consult an injected opt-in gate shouldNotify(projectRoot) before constructing ANY notification (individual or digest) and MUST be fully inert (construct no Notification) when the gate denies.' The individual path consults shouldNotify at admit() (line 143). The DIGEST path does not: shouldNotify is consulted only when a root is BUFFERED (admit line 152), never again at emit time — closeWindow() (line 176-180) calls notifyDigest unconditionally whenever bufferedRoots.size>0. Because the gate is app-wide and live-refreshed via the quickSave mirror, a user who MUTES after roots were buffered but before the window closes still gets the pending digest: the mute is ignored for that digest. This race is normally <=COALESCE_WINDOW_MS, but combined with FINDING-013 (closeWindow only fires on the next transition/dispose) it can widen to arbitrarily long — the stale digest fires whenever the next transition eventually arrives, well after the user muted. Impact is low (read-only, one extra digest toast), but it is a genuine inertness leak, and the frozen gate-inertness tests never exercise a gate flip BETWEEN buffering and digest emit (TEST-546/547 hold shouldNotify constant), so the gap is untested.
fix: Re-consult the app-wide gate at digest construction: guard notifyDigest in closeWindow() with shouldNotify (for an app-wide mute a projectRoot-agnostic check suffices, e.g. gate any buffered root or expose a global-enabled read), so a mute that lands before window close suppresses the pending digest too. Add a test that flips shouldNotify false after buffering and asserts closeWindow()/flush() emits no digest.
resolution: RESOLVED against current code. closeWindow (orky-needs-you-notifier.ts:228-238) now RE-CONSULTS the gate at digest-emit time: const allowed = [...this.bufferedRoots].filter(root => this.deps.shouldNotify(root)); it emits notifyDigest(digestCopy(allowed.length)) only when allowed.length > 0. So a mute landing AFTER roots are buffered but BEFORE window close suppresses the pending digest, closing the inertness leak; the individual path already gates at admit (:168). flush() routes through closeWindow (:263-266), so the forced-boundary and armed-timer paths are re-gated too. The digest count stays correct: allowed.length equals the buffered count when nothing was muted (TEST-539 count 3; TEST-547 denied-at-admit count 1). Pinned by TEST-578 (buffer p1..p4 while enabled, mute before close -> flush() emits NO digest; re-enable + close empty -> still nothing). Non-blocking LOW cleared.

claim: The FINDING-005 fix declares setTimer? and clearTimer? as two INDEPENDENTLY-optional deps. Production (register.ts:138-139) correctly supplies BOTH, so current behavior is right and this is not a shipped-runtime defect. But the type shape permits a half-configuration (setTimer supplied, clearTimer omitted) that is silently WRONG: clearFlushTimer() calls this.deps.clearTimer?.(handle) (optional-chained to a no-op) then nulls this.flushTimer WITHOUT cancelling the underlying real timer. On a window ROLL the stale real timer would still fire and closeWindow() the fresh (now-current) window prematurely; and the callback's this.flushTimer = null would clobber the newly-armed handle. The optionality exists only so the frozen mirror test can construct a timer-less notifier (a caller that never buffers a digest); coupling the pair as all-or-nothing would make the timer-less seam explicit while making the dangerous half-configuration unrepresentable.
fix: Make the timer scheduler an all-or-nothing pair: a single optional object dep (e.g. scheduler?: { set: (fn, ms) => handle; clear: (handle) => void }) so a caller cannot supply one without the other, or assert both-or-neither in the constructor. Keeps the timer-less mirror seam (omit the whole object) while making setTimer-without-clearTimer unrepresentable. propose CONV: paired arm/cancel (or acquire/release) lifecycle dependencies MUST be injected as a single all-or-nothing unit (one object, or a both-or-neither constructor check), never as two independently-optional fields, so a half-supplied pair that leaks or misfires the resource cannot type-check.
